title: Reading and Use of English - Part 7
description: You are going to read an article about four people who have lived or worked in a foreign country. For questions 43-52, choose from the people (A-D). The people may be chosen more than once.

content.title: A Life Abroad: Four People on Living in Another Country

texts.A:
### Nathan, 31, Civil Engineer
I moved to Singapore for a two-year contract and ended up staying for five years. [44]Having international project experience on my CV opened doors that would simply not have existed had I stayed at home - several of my current clients specifically sought out engineers with cross-border experience.[/44] The work itself is more varied here, partly because the region is undergoing rapid infrastructure development. [50]The range of projects I work on is considerably broader than it would have been in my own country, which has accelerated my professional development in ways I did not expect when I first arrived.[/50] I am bilingual now, which also helps, and the lifestyle suits me.
texts.B:
### Priya, 27, Secondary School Teacher
[45]I underestimated how difficult the first year abroad would be - I had expected the cultural adjustment, but the loneliness was something else entirely, and there were months when I genuinely considered going home.[/45] Teaching in Germany requires navigating a very different school system and a different philosophy of education. But I persevered and eventually settled in well. [47]I have actually decided to move back to the UK at the end of this academic year, not because Germany has been a negative experience but because I miss my family and feel ready for the next stage of my life at home.[/47] [52]That said, I would encourage any young teacher to spend at least a year abroad - the professional confidence and adaptability you gain are invaluable.[/52]
texts.C:
### Diana, 45, Restaurant Owner
[43]Living in Japan for six years gave me a completely different perspective on the food culture I grew up with in Italy - I became much more critical of some traditions I had always taken for granted, and also much more appreciative of others.[/43] I now run an Italian restaurant in Tokyo, which allows me to bring the two cultures together in a way I find personally and professionally fulfilling. [48]I attend a Japanese tea ceremony class every Saturday morning, which keeps me grounded in the community and prevents me from becoming entirely absorbed in my own expatriate circle.[/48] It is a deliberate choice. [51]The biggest sacrifice has been distance from my parents, who are now elderly, and the guilt about not being nearby when they need support has never fully disappeared.[/51]
texts.D:
### Stefan, 38, IT Consultant
[46]When I first moved to Brazil, I could not speak Portuguese at all, and the effort of becoming fluent out of sheer necessity made me a genuinely better communicator in all areas of my professional life - not just in language, but in listening and adapting.[/46] The tech sector here has grown enormously. [49]When I arrived ten years ago, there was a degree of scepticism towards foreign professionals in local companies, but attitudes have shifted considerably and international experience is now more likely to be seen as an asset than a threat.[/49] The market is much more open-minded than it used to be, and that has made my work here feel more respected.

questions.question: found that living abroad changed the way they view their own culture?
questions.explanation: Diana says living in Japan gave her a completely different perspective on Italian food culture, making her both more critical and more appreciative.

questions.question: credits their time abroad with giving them a career advantage?
questions.explanation: Nathan says international project experience opened doors that would not have existed had he stayed at home.

questions.question: describes the early period of living abroad as more difficult than they had anticipated?
questions.explanation: Priya says she underestimated how difficult the first year would be and that the loneliness was something else entirely.

questions.question: developed a new skill as a direct result of a practical challenge faced abroad?
questions.explanation: Stefan became fluent in Portuguese out of sheer necessity, which also made him a better communicator overall.

questions.question: has chosen to return to their home country at least for the time being?
questions.explanation: Priya has decided to move back to the UK at the end of the academic year because she misses her family.

questions.question: maintains a regular connection with the local culture through a specific activity?
questions.explanation: Diana attends a Japanese tea ceremony class every Saturday morning to stay connected to the community.

questions.question: notices that attitudes towards people from other countries in their field have changed?
questions.explanation: Stefan says that scepticism towards foreign professionals has shifted and international experience is now seen as an asset.

questions.question: has benefited professionally from having a greater variety of work available to them?
questions.explanation: Nathan says the range of projects he works on is considerably broader than it would have been at home.

questions.question: mentions a personal cost that comes with living far from family?
questions.explanation: Diana says the biggest sacrifice has been distance from her elderly parents and the guilt about not being nearby.

questions.question: recommends that people in their profession consider spending time working abroad?
questions.explanation: Priya would encourage any young teacher to spend at least a year abroad for the professional confidence and adaptability gained.
